children.name: Cuculus
children.description: Obligate brood parasites that lay their eggs in the nests of other species; each female specialises on one host and lays mimetic eggs that match the host's clutch. The cuckoo's descending two-note call is one of the iconic sounds of Swedish spring, heard from reed beds, farmland, and forest edge.

children.name: Cuculus canorus
children.description: The cuckoo, common cuckoo, European cuckoo or Eurasian cuckoo is a member of the cuckoo order of birds, Cuculiformes, which includes the roadrunners, the anis and the coucals.

children.name: Cuculus claudius
children.description: A recently recognised species split from C. saturatus; breeds in the Himalayas and winters in Southeast Asia. Its call is a slower, deeper version of the common cuckoo.

children.name: Cuculus fuggi
children.description: A cuckoo of the Himalayan and Chinese mountain forests, recently split from C. saturatus. Its song is a deep, trisyllabic 'hoo-hoo-hoo' that descends in pitch.

children.name: Cuculus vagrans
children.description: A distinctive and well-known species within its range. It inhabits a variety of habitats from lowland forests to mountain highlands, feeding on insects, seeds, and fruits.

children.name: Hierococcyx
children.description: Hawk-cuckoos of Asia, named for their striking resemblance to Accipiter hawks in plumage and flight—a form of Batesian mimicry that may help them access nests. They are famed for extraordinarily loud, repetitive calls that build to a fever pitch in breeding season.

children.name: Cacomantis
children.description: Small to medium cuckoos of Australasia and Southeast Asia, several with hepatic (rufous) female morphs that resemble hawks. Most are brood parasites of warblers and flycatchers and are notable for their plaintive, cascading calls.

children.name: Chrysococcyx
children.description: Jewel-cuckoos of the Old World tropics, many adorned with iridescent bronze, green, or purple plumage. All are brood parasites; their small size lets them exploit the nests of tiny warblers and sunbirds that larger cuckoos cannot access.

children.name: Chrysococcyx xyrus
children.description: A recently described bronze cuckoo from the Solomon Islands. The male has a rufous throat patch unique among Chrysococcyx.

children.name: Chrysococcyx torotoro
children.description: A small bronze-cuckoo widespread from New Guinea through the Bismarck Archipelago and Solomon Islands, recognisable by its yellow bill and shining green upperparts. It parasitises gerygones in lowland forest and secondary growth.

children.name: Clamator
children.description: Brood-parasitic cuckoos of the Old World, with long tails and crested heads.

children.name: Coccyzus
children.description: New World cuckoos of the Americas, slender birds with long graduated tails often featuring bold black-and-white tips. Unlike most Old World cuckoos, the majority raise their own young, though some are known to occasionally parasitise other nests.

children.name: Coccyzus americanus
children.description: The yellow-billed cuckoo is a member of the cuckoo family. Common folk names for this bird in the southern United States are rain crow and storm crow. These likely refer to the bird's habit of calling on hot days, often presaging rain showers or thunderstorms.

children.name: Geococcyx
children.description: Terrestrial cuckoos of arid North and Central America, built for running with long legs, a long tail, and a heavy bill. They are non-parasitic, raising their own young, and are apex predators of small vertebrates in desert scrub.

children.name: Neomorphus
children.description: Ground-cuckoos of Central and South American rainforests, non-parasitic birds that follow columns of army ants to snatch flushed insects and small vertebrates. They are among the most sought-after birds for visiting naturalists due to their rarity and secretive habits.

children.name: Crotophaga
children.description: Anis of the Americas — glossy black, communally breeding cuckoos that live in social groups and share a single nest for incubation. They are non-parasitic and typically inhabit open grasslands, pastures, and tropical scrub.

children.name: Guira
children.description: A monotypic genus of South America containing the extravagantly crested Guira Cuckoo. It is non-parasitic and breeds communally like the anis, though it often removes eggs laid by other females in the shared nest.

children.name: Centropus
children.description: Coucals — large, non-parasitic cuckoos with long tails, short rounded wings, and powerful legs. They are found across sub-Saharan Africa, South Asia, and Australasia, where they hunt insects, lizards, and small mammals in dense vegetation.

children.name: Centropus phasianus
children.description: The Australian representative of the genus, found across northern and eastern Australia. It behaves like a pheasant, skulking through long grass and dense vegetation. Its call is a series of deep, hollow notes that descend in pitch like a bubbling liquid being poured from a large bottle — a distinctly eerie sound of the Australian bush.

children.name: Centropus giasianus
children.description: A coucal of the Lesser Sunda Islands in Indonesia. Little is known of its ecology but it occupies dry forest and scrub habitats.

children.name: Centropus chalybius
children.description: A coucal endemic to the northern Moluccas of Indonesia. Its iridescent blue-green head is distinctive.

children.name: Centropus ansorgii
children.description: A distinctive and well-known species within its range. It inhabits a variety of habitats from lowland forests to mountain highlands, feeding on insects, seeds, and fruits.

children.name: Centropus medius
children.description: A medium-sized coucal of the Malay Peninsula and Borneo, inhabiting secondary forest, scrub, and forest edge. It is intermediate in size between the large Greater Coucal and small Lesser Coucal and can be distinguished by its bay-brown plumage and barred tail.

children.name: Centropus epomidis
children.description: A forest coucal of the Congo Basin rainforest, particularly associated with the Ituri Forest of the Democratic Republic of Congo. It inhabits dense understorey and ground-level vegetation in primary lowland forest, its large size enabling it to tackle small vertebrate prey.

children.name: Centropus woodfordii
children.description: A large coucal of the Solomon Islands with a distinctive white head and neck contrasting with dark wings and body. It inhabits forest edge and secondary scrub and is one of the dominant large ground-hunting birds of the Solomon archipelago.

children.name: Eudynamys
children.description: Large fruit-eating cuckoos with striking sexual dimorphism (males glossy black, females barred brown). Brood parasites of large passerines.

children.name: Phaenicophaeus
children.description: Non-parasitic cuckoos of South and Southeast Asian forests, with long graduated tails, colourful bare facial skin, and heavy, decurved bills. They creep through dense vegetation hunting large insects, lizards, and chameleons, and build their own nests.

children.name: Coua
children.description: Non-parasitic cuckoos endemic to Madagascar, with long tails, short rounded wings, and often strikingly coloured bare facial skin. Ground-couas walk through forest floor leaf litter hunting invertebrates and small vertebrates; arboreal species feed on fruit and insects in the canopy.

children.name: Surniculus
children.description: Brood-parasitic cuckoos of South and Southeast Asia that closely mimic drongos (Dicrurus) in plumage, behaviour, and even the fork of the tail in some species — thought to provide Batesian mimicry that either intimidates hosts or allows the cuckoo to approach host nests undetected.

children.name: Surniculus musschenbroeki
children.description: #REDIRECT Moluccan drongo-cuckoo Category:Taxa named by Adolf Bernhard Meyer.

children.name: Piaya
children.description: Long-tailed, non-parasitic cuckoos of Central and South America named for their squirrel-like habit of running along large branches and bounding through the forest canopy. They are chestnut above and grey below, with a long, broadly graduated, black-and-white tail.

children.name: Ceuthmochares
children.description: Secretive African forest cuckoos with long tails, short rounded wings, and a vivid yellow bill that is often the only feature visible as the bird creeps through dense understorey vegetation. They are non-parasitic, raising their own young in open cup nests.

children.name: Dromococcyx
children.description: Secretive brood-parasitic cuckoos of South American forest floor, with long, ornate tails and a distinctive crest. They skulk through dense understorey and are most often detected by voice, with loud, repetitive calls that carry through the forest at dusk and dawn.

children.name: Tapera
children.description: A monotypic genus for the Striped Cuckoo — a brood-parasitic cuckoo of open habitats in the Americas with a conspicuous erectile crest and a loud, far-carrying, repetitive call. Unlike most brood parasites it favours open grasslands and scrub rather than dense forest.

children.name: Tapera naevia
children.description: The striped cuckoo is a near-passerine bird, the only member of the genus Tapera. This resident cuckoo is found from Mexico and Trinidad south to Bolivia and Argentina and Colombia.

children.name: Carpococcyx
children.description: Large, mainly terrestrial cuckoos of Southeast Asian primary rainforest, with massive decurved bills, long tails, and striking bare facial skin. They walk through the forest floor hunting large insects, lizards, snakes, and small mammals. Non-parasitic and very rarely seen.

children.name: Dasylophus
children.description: Non-parasitic malkohas endemic to the Philippines, with conspicuous, filamentous crests and long tails. They creep through forest canopy vegetation hunting large insects, lizards, and fruit.

children.name: Saurothera
children.description: Non-parasitic cuckoos of the Greater Antilles, each island having its own species. As their name implies, lizards are a major part of their diet; they also take insects and small vertebrates. They are large-bodied with long tails and powerful, slightly decurved bills.
